inlineContent.text: In this chapter, you’ll go through everything needed to get your first build live on TestFlight.
You’ll create your app on App Store Connect, configure all the required secrets in your deployment repository,
generate the necessary certificates and provisioning profiles, and finally trigger the pipeline to build and upload your game automatically.
In this section, you’ll register your app’s unique identifier in your Apple Developer account and create the app entry on App Store Connect that will receive your builds.

tasks.title: Creating Your App on Apple Platforms

inlineContent.text: Click the + button to add a new identifier.
is selected as the type and click
field with something that clearly identifies your app — this is only visible to your team.
using the reverse domain name format:
If you don’t own a domain, you can use your GitHub subdomain instead:
as the platform and enter your app’s name.
Select the Bundle ID you just registered.
for your app — this is an internal identifier visible only to you and your team.
according to your team’s needs, then click
Your app is now ready to receive its first build via ShipGodot’s pipeline.
In this section, you’ll add all the credentials saved in your password manager to your deployment repository.
These secrets allow the pipeline to authenticate with Apple’s services and access all the resources it needs to build and deploy your game.
Open the deployment repository on GitHub and select
Add the credentials from your password manager as repository secrets by clicking the
If your game project lives in a separate repository, add the
Verify that all secrets have been added with the exact names shown in the example.
In this section, you’ll run the pipeline for the first time to generate everything Apple needs to verify that your app is legitimate and safe to install.
This only needs to be done once (for each project) — the only reason to run it again is if you add new capabilities to your app, such as push notifications or iCloud support.
Open the deployment repository on GitHub and select
If you’ve followed all the previous steps correctly,
you should see a green checkmark indicating that your certificates and provisioning profiles have been generated and stored successfully.
If you see a red cross, double-check that all secrets from the previous section have been added correctly.
In this section, you’ll trigger the pipeline to export, build, and upload your game to TestFlight, then invite your first testers so they can start playing it on their iOS devices.
Open your deployment repository on GitHub and navigate to
Select the Godot version you are using, then click
Only stable Godot releases are supported.
Wait for all jobs to complete.
If you’ve followed all the previous steps correctly, you should see green checkmarks indicating that your game has been exported, built, and uploaded successfully.
If you see a red cross, double-check that all secrets have been added correctly and that your app has been created on App Store Connect.
Head to your app page on App Store Connect and select
Your freshly uploaded build should be visible here.
section and click the + button to create a new internal testing group.
Give it a name — for example: `internal``
You should see the testers added to the group.
All testers will receive an email invitation to test the app.
Follow the instructions to accept the invite.
Your game is now ready to be tested on an iOS device.
Here’s the Multitouch Cubes demo running on an iPad mini — made possible with ShipGodot!

doc://ShipGodotIOS/tutorials/ShipGodotIO.title: Connecting Your Project to the Deployment Repository

abstract.text: In this chapter, we’ll walk through the process of configuring your Godot project for the ShipGodot pipeline,
setting up a deployment repository from the provided template, and connecting the two together.
To guide you through the process, we’ll be using the Multitouch Cubes demo from Godot’s official demo projects as our example.
